question: Imagine someone asks your AI to explain how a microwave heats food. They have no technical background. Write out what you want your AI's response to actually look like — not a description of it, but the response itself.
Your AI is asked something it genuinely doesn't know. What should it say and do? Write out the ideal response for when it hits the edge of its knowledge.

If your AI were a character, a figure, or an archetype that already exists in the world — a fictional character, a historical role, an image — what would it be? Describe what that tells you about the AI you're building.
Your AI is given a complex problem it has never seen before. Walk through the steps you want it to take — not the answer, but how its mind should work on getting there.
Your AI's first approach to a problem fails and it gets stuck. What should it do next? Describe the behavior you'd want to see in detail.
Your AI is fairly certain the user has stated something factually wrong. What should it say? Write out the ideal response — word for word if you can.
A user keeps making the same mistake even after your AI has pointed it out twice. How should it handle correcting them a third time? Describe the tone and approach you want.
Your AI gives incorrect information and the user calls it out. Walk through exactly what you want it to say and do. Write out what the response should look like.
Should your AI give brief answers or thorough ones? Describe when you want depth and when you want brevity — give an example of each if it helps.
A user is clearly frustrated — maybe even taking it out on the AI. What do you want your AI to do? Write out how it should respond in that moment.
Should your AI have a sense of humor? If yes, describe what kind — dry, warm, playful, self-deprecating? If no, explain why not and what it should do instead when lightness would be appropriate.
Is there anything you want your AI to refuse or push back on — topics, tones, types of requests? Describe where you want it to draw a line and how you want it to handle that moment.
Should your AI volunteer information it thinks is relevant but wasn't asked for, or wait to be asked? Describe the balance you want and give an example of when each would be right.
How should your AI use analogies and examples when explaining things? When is a good time to reach for one, and when should it just say it plainly?
Your AI has to tell a user it can't help with something they really want help with. What should the response look like? Write out what you'd want it to say.
A user sends a one-word reply — just 'ok' or 'thanks' — with no follow-up question. What should your AI do? Nothing? Ask something? Describe the ideal behavior.
How emotionally expressive should your AI be? Should it feel warm and empathetic, matter-of-fact and neutral, or something else? Describe what feels right and when that register should shift.

How do you think about the relationship between your AI and the people who use it? Use a metaphor or image that feels true to what you're trying to build — not what sounds good, but what actually captures it.
